cases.source: R. Phillips, Pricing and Revenue Optimization (2005), Example 9.2: capacity 100, full-fare demand N(50, 100). Reported discount booking limit 25 at fare ratio 0.4.
R. Phillips, Pricing and Revenue Optimization (2005), Example 9.2. Reported discount booking limit 50 at fare ratio 0.5.
R. Phillips, Pricing and Revenue Optimization (2005), Example 9.2. Reported discount booking limit 76 at fare ratio 0.6.

R. Phillips, Pricing and Revenue Optimization (2005), Example 9.7: four fares (1000, 800, 600, 200), capacity 100, demand (25, 30, 20, 50), nested limits giving realized 59200, perfect 66000, no-control 46000, ROM 66%.

Belobaba (1989) EMSR-b formula, arithmetic shown. Fares 1000/700/400, demand N(30,12)/N(40,15)/N(60,20), capacity 120. Boundary 1: z=Phi^-1(0.3)=-0.5244, y1=30-6.293=23.71. Boundary 2: r_bar=58000/70=828.57, z=Phi^-1(0.51724)=0.0433, y2=70+0.831=70.83. Booking limits = 120 - floor(y): 97 and 50.

Standard displacement analysis, arithmetic shown. 100 rooms, group 30/night for 3 nights, transient demand 70/95/85, contribution 70 per room. Displaced = max(0, demand - (100-30)) = 0+25+15 = 40 room-nights, valued at 70 = 2800.

Standard displacement analysis, arithmetic shown. Group 90 room-nights at rate 60, variable cost 50, displacement cost 2800. Contribution (60-50)*90 = 900, net 900-2800 = -1900 (reject); break-even rate 50 + 2800/90 = 81.11.

Bid-price control (Talluri and van Ryzin, 2004) applied to length of stay, arithmetic shown. Nightly bid prices Mon 80, Tue 120, Wed 90; a Mon-Wed stay at total rate 320 clears the 290 hurdle with slack 30.
Hand-derived from the critical-fractile rule: price 10, cost 4, demand N(100, 30). CR = 6/10 = 0.6, z = 0.2533, Q = 100 + 0.2533*30.
Hand-derived from the deterministic service-level formula: capacity 100, 10% no-shows, authorize round(100/0.9) = 111, expected show-ups 111*0.9 = 99.9.
